docs.title: Where Technology Meets Hands-On Innovation
docs.text: This is a space dedicated to exploring the intersection of code, creativity, and curiosity. Whether you're interested in building with Arduino, growing something green, automating your digital life, or diving into the latest AI breakthroughs, you'll find guides, examples, and insights here.

docs.title: 🤖 Arduino Projects
docs.text: From microcontroller fundamentals to connected IoT devices, explore practical electronics projects that bring ideas to life. Learn how to interface sensors, control actuators, and prototype innovative hardware solutions.

docs.title: 🌱 Gardening & Growing
docs.text: Combine nature with technology. Discover automation techniques, monitoring systems, and cultivation guides. Whether it's smart irrigation or optimizing plant growth, we bridge the gap between horticulture and innovation.

docs.title: 🏠 Home Lab
docs.text: Build your own infrastructure. Set up servers, experiment with self-hosting, and create a personal testing ground for new technologies. Your home lab is where experimentation happens without limits.

docs.title: ⚙️ Automation
docs.text: Streamline repetitive tasks and maximize efficiency. From DevOps workflows to home automation scripts, learn how to work smarter. Includes git workflows, CI/CD pipelines, and intelligent system design.

docs.title: 🧠 AI & Machine Learning
docs.text: Explore artificial intelligence from concepts to implementation. Understand large language models, prompt engineering, agent design, and practical AI applications in real-world scenarios.

docs.title: About This Site
docs.text:
Built with MkDocs and the Material theme, this documentation site is designed for clarity, searchability, and modern aesthetics. Everything is open, version-controlled, and automatically deployed through GitHub Actions.

Let's build, grow, and automate together.

Code and Camera: Where curiosity drives creation.

docs.text: Site management, documentation, and deployment resources.

docs.title: Welcome to MkDocs
docs.text: This is the homepage for the Code and Camera documentation site. For full MkDocs documentation visit mkdocs.org.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
• mkdocs new [dir-name] - Create a new project.
  • mkdocs serve - Start the live-reloading docs server.
  • mkdocs build - Build the documentation site.
  • mkdocs -h - Print help message and exit.

docs.title: Code Blocks in Content Tabs
MkDocs Configuration Example
docs.text:
Reference example of a complete MkDocs configuration with Material theme, multiple extensions, and plugins.

YAML
# Project information
site_name: Complete MkDocs Example
site_url: https://example.com/
site_author: Your Name
site_description: A comprehensive example of MkDocs functionality

# Repository
repo_name: username/repo
repo_url: https://github.com/username/repo
edit_uri: edit/main/docs/

# Theme configuration
theme:
       name: material
       features:
              - navigation.tabs
              - navigation.sections
              - navigation.top
              - search.highlight
       palette:
              - scheme: default
                     primary: indigo
                     accent: indigo
                     toggle:
                            icon: material/brightness-7
                            name: Switch to dark mode
              - scheme: slate
                     primary: indigo
                     accent: indigo
                     toggle:
                            icon: material/brightness-4
                            name: Switch to light mode

# Extensions
markdown_extensions:
       - admonition
       - codehilite
       - footnotes
       - toc:
                     permalink: true
       - pymdownx.highlight
       - pymdownx.superfences
       - pymdownx.tasklist:
                     custom_checkbox: true

# Navigation
nav:
       - Home: index.md
       - User Guide:
              - Installation: guide/installation.md
              - Configuration: guide/configuration.md
       - API Reference:
              - Authentication: api/auth.md
              - Endpoints: api/endpoints.md
       - About: about.md

# Extra settings
extra:
       social:
              - icon: fontawesome/brands/github
                     link: https://github.com/username
              - icon: fontawesome/brands/twitter
                     link: https://twitter.com/username

# Plugins
plugins:
       - search
       - minify:
                     minify_html: true

  • Prompt engineering

Harness the power of AI to solve problems and create intelligent solutions.

docs.title: Building a Local AI Chatbot with Ollama
docs.text: Run AI models on your own hardware—private, free, and surprisingly capable.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Ollama makes it easy to run large language models (LLMs) locally. No API keys, no cloud costs, no data leaving your machine. This guide walks you through setting up Ollama and creating a simple chatbot interface.

docs.title: Project Details
docs.text: Detail Information Difficulty Intermediate Time Required 1 hour Category AI & Machine Learning Last Updated December 2025

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• Installing and configuring Ollama
  • Downloading and running various AI models
  • Creating a Python chatbot interface
  • Understanding model sizes and hardware requirements
  • Customizing model behavior with system prompts

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Windows 11, macOS, or Linux
  • 8GB+ RAM (16GB+ recommended)
  • 10GB+ free disk space per model
  • Basic command line familiarity
  • Python 3.8+ (for the chatbot interface)

docs.title: Hardware Recommendations
docs.text: RAM GPU VRAM Recommended Models 8GB None Phi-3, Gemma 2B, TinyLlama 16GB None Llama 3.2 3B, Mistral 7B 16GB 8GB+ Llama 3.1 8B, CodeLlama 32GB+ 12GB+ Llama 3.1 70B (quantized), Mixtral

docs.title: Step 1: Install Ollama
docs.text:
WindowsmacOSLinux
  1. Download from ollama.com/download
  2. Run the installer
  3. Ollama runs as a background service automatically
Bash
# Using Homebrew
brew install ollama

# Or download from ollama.com/download
Bash
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh

docs.title: Step 2: Verify Installation
docs.text:
Bash
# Check Ollama is running
ollama --version

# Should show something like: ollama version 0.1.x

docs.title: Step 3: Download Your First Model
docs.text:
Bash
# Download Llama 3.2 (3B parameters - good balance of speed/quality)
ollama pull llama3.2

# Other popular options:
# ollama pull mistral        # Great general purpose 7B model
# ollama pull codellama      # Optimized for code
# ollama pull phi3           # Microsoft's small but capable model
# ollama pull gemma2:2b      # Google's efficient 2B model

Download Sizes

  • phi3: ~2.5GB
  • llama3.2: ~2GB
  • mistral: ~4GB
  • llama3.1:8b: ~4.7GB
  • codellama: ~4GB

docs.title: Step 4: Test in Terminal
docs.text:
Bash
# Start an interactive chat
ollama run llama3.2

# You'll see a prompt:
>>> Hello! What can you help me with?

# Type /bye to exit

docs.title: Step 5: Create a Python Chatbot
docs.text:
First, install the required package:

Bash
pip install ollama

Now create your chatbot:

chatbot.py
"""
Local AI Chatbot using Ollama
A simple but powerful conversational interface.
"""

import ollama
from datetime import datetime

# Configuration
MODEL = "llama3.2"  # Change to your preferred model
SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a helpful, friendly AI assistant. You are:
- Concise but thorough in your responses
- Honest about limitations and uncertainties
- Focused on being genuinely helpful

Current date: {date}
"""

def create_chat():
    """Initialize a new chat session with conversation history."""
    return {
        "messages": [],
        "system": SYSTEM_PROMPT.format(date=datetime.now().strftime("%B %d, %Y"))
    }

def chat(session: dict, user_message: str) -> str:
    """
    Send a message and get a response while maintaining conversation history.

    Args:
        session: Chat session dictionary with message history
        user_message: The user's input message

    Returns:
        The AI's response text
    """
    # Add user message to history
    session["messages"].append({
        "role": "user",
        "content": user_message
    })

    # Prepare messages with system prompt
    messages = [
        {"role": "system", "content": session["system"]},
        *session["messages"]
    ]

    # Get response from Ollama
    response = ollama.chat(
        model=MODEL,
        messages=messages,
        options={
            "temperature": 0.7,     # Creativity (0.0-1.0)
            "top_p": 0.9,           # Nucleus sampling
            "num_predict": 1024,    # Max tokens in response
        }
    )

    # Extract and store assistant response
    assistant_message = response["message"]["content"]
    session["messages"].append({
        "role": "assistant", 
        "content": assistant_message
    })

    return assistant_message

def stream_chat(session: dict, user_message: str):
    """
    Stream a response token by token for a more interactive feel.

    Args:
        session: Chat session dictionary
        user_message: The user's input message

    Yields:
        Individual tokens as they're generated
    """
    session["messages"].append({
        "role": "user",
        "content": user_message
    })

    messages = [
        {"role": "system", "content": session["system"]},
        *session["messages"]
    ]

    # Stream the response
    full_response = ""
    for chunk in ollama.chat(
        model=MODEL,
        messages=messages,
        stream=True,
        options={"temperature": 0.7}
    ):
        token = chunk["message"]["content"]
        full_response += token
        yield token

    # Store complete response in history
    session["messages"].append({
        "role": "assistant",
        "content": full_response
    })

def main():
    """Main chat loop with streaming responses."""
    print("=" * 50)
    print(f"  Local AI Chatbot (Model: {MODEL})")
    print("=" * 50)
    print("Type 'quit' to exit, 'clear' to reset conversation\n")

    session = create_chat()

    while True:
        try:
            user_input = input("\n🧑 You: ").strip()

            if not user_input:
                continue

            if user_input.lower() == "quit":
                print("\nGoodbye! 👋")
                break

            if user_input.lower() == "clear":
                session = create_chat()
                print("\n🔄 Conversation cleared!")
                continue

            # Stream the response
            print("\n🤖 AI: ", end="", flush=True)
            for token in stream_chat(session, user_input):
                print(token, end="", flush=True)
            print()  # New line after response

        except KeyboardInterrupt:
            print("\n\nGoodbye! 👋")
            break
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"\n❌ Error: {e}")
            print("Make sure Ollama is running: `ollama serve`")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

docs.title: Step 6: Run Your Chatbot
docs.text:
Bash
python chatbot.py

docs.title: Create a Custom Model with System Prompt
docs.text:
Create a Modelfile:

Modelfile
FROM llama3.2

# Set the system prompt
SYSTEM """
You are CodeHelper, an expert programming assistant. You:
- Write clean, well-documented code
- Explain concepts clearly with examples
- Suggest best practices and optimizations
- Ask clarifying questions when requirements are unclear
"""

# Adjust parameters
PARAMETER temperature 0.3
PARAMETER top_p 0.9

Build and use your custom model:

Bash
# Create the custom model
ollama create codehelper -f Modelfile

# Run it
ollama run codehelper

docs.title: Useful API Examples
docs.text:
Python
# List available models
models = ollama.list()
print([m["name"] for m in models["models"]])

# Get model info
info = ollama.show("llama3.2")
print(f"Parameters: {info['details']['parameter_size']}")

# Generate text (no conversation)
response = ollama.generate(
    model="llama3.2",
    prompt="Write a haiku about programming:"
)
print(response["response"])

# Embeddings for semantic search
embedding = ollama.embeddings(
    model="llama3.2",
    prompt="What is machine learning?"
)
print(f"Embedding dimensions: {len(embedding['embedding'])}")

docs.title: Model Comparison
docs.text: Model Size Speed Best For phi3 2.5GB ⚡⚡⚡ Quick tasks, low resources gemma2:2b 1.6GB ⚡⚡⚡ Efficient, Google quality llama3.2 2GB ⚡⚡⚡ General purpose, balanced mistral 4GB ⚡⚡ Excellent reasoning llama3.1:8b 4.7GB ⚡⚡ High quality, versatile codellama 4GB ⚡⚡ Code generation mixtral 26GB ⚡ Best open-source quality

docs.title: Ollama not responding
docs.text:
Bash
# Check if service is running
ollama serve

# Or restart the service
# Windows: Restart from system tray
# macOS/Linux: `sudo systemctl restart ollama`

docs.title: Out of memory
docs.text:
• Try a smaller model (phi3, gemma2:2b)
  • Close other applications
  • Use quantized versions (e.g., llama3.1:8b-q4_0)

docs.title: Slow responses
docs.text:
• Ensure GPU is being used (check Task Manager/Activity Monitor)
  • Try a smaller model
  • Reduce num_predict in options

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Build a web interface with Gradio or Streamlit
  • Add RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) with your own documents
  • Create specialized assistants for different tasks
  • Experiment with vision models (LLaVA)
  • Set up Ollama on your home server for network access

docs.title: Arduino Projects
docs.text: Explore practical electronics and microcontroller projects. Learn how to design, build, and program Arduino-based solutions for real-world applications.

docs.title: Topics Covered
docs.text:
• Microcontroller fundamentals
  • Sensor integration and interfacing
  • Actuator control
  • IoT device development
  • Circuit design and prototyping
  • Arduino libraries and frameworks
  • Project examples and tutorials

Getting started with Arduino? Begin with basic LED projects and work your way up to complex IoT systems.

docs.title: LED Blink with Button Control
docs.text: A classic starter project that teaches digital input/output fundamentals.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Learn how to read a button press and control an LED. This foundational project introduces you to digital pins, pull-up resistors, and basic circuit design.

docs.title: Project Details
docs.text: Detail Information Difficulty Beginner Time Required 30 minutes Category Arduino Last Updated December 2025

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• Reading digital input from a push button
  • Controlling LED output with digitalWrite()
  • Understanding pull-up resistors
  • Basic breadboard wiring

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Arduino IDE installed
  • Basic understanding of circuits
  • Familiarity with breadboards

docs.title: Hardware
docs.text: Component Quantity Notes Arduino Uno 1 Or compatible board LED (any color) 1 5mm standard Push Button 1 Momentary switch 220Ω Resistor 1 For LED 10kΩ Resistor 1 For button (optional with INPUT_PULLUP) Breadboard 1 Half-size or larger Jumper Wires 6+ Male-to-male

docs.title: Software
docs.text: • Arduino IDE 2.0+

docs.title: Step 1: Set Up the Circuit
docs.text:
1. Place the LED on the breadboard
  2. Connect the longer leg (anode) through a 220Ω resistor to pin 13
  3. Connect the shorter leg (cathode) to GND
  4. Place the push button across the breadboard gap
  5. Connect one side to pin 2, the other to GND

docs.title: Step 2: Write the Code
Step 3: Upload and Test
docs.text:
1. Connect your Arduino via USB
  2. Select the correct board and port in Arduino IDE
  3. Click Upload
  4. Press the button - the LED should light up!

docs.title: Enhancements to Try
LED doesn't light up
docs.text:
• Check LED orientation (longer leg = positive)
  • Verify resistor value (220Ω)
  • Confirm pin connections

docs.title: Button doesn't respond
docs.text:
• Ensure INPUT_PULLUP is used or external pull-up resistor is connected
  • Check button orientation on breadboard

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Add multiple LEDs for a traffic light
  • Use PWM for LED brightness control
  • Add a buzzer for audio feedback
  • Create a reaction time game

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Arduino Digital Pins
  • Pull-up Resistors Explained

docs.title: Automated Backup Script with PowerShell
docs.text: Never lose important files again with this automated backup solution.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This project creates a PowerShell script that automatically backs up specified folders to a destination of your choice—local drive, network share, or cloud-synced folder. Set it and forget it with Windows Task Scheduler.

docs.title: Project Details
docs.text: Detail Information Difficulty Beginner Time Required 30-45 minutes Category Automation Last Updated December 2025

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• PowerShell scripting basics
  • File operations and error handling
  • Logging and notifications
  • Windows Task Scheduler automation
  • Incremental vs full backup strategies

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Windows 10/11 or Windows Server
  • PowerShell 5.1+ (included with Windows)
  • Administrator access for Task Scheduler

docs.title: Step 1: Create the Backup Script
docs.text:
Create a new file called backup-script.ps1:

backup-script.ps1
#Requires -Version 5.1
<#
.SYNOPSIS
    Automated backup script for important files and folders.
.DESCRIPTION
    Creates timestamped backups with logging and optional email notifications.
.AUTHOR
    Code and Camera
.VERSION
    1.0.0
#>

# ============================================
# CONFIGURATION - Modify these settings
# ============================================

$Config = @{
    # Folders to backup (add your paths here)
    SourceFolders = @(
        "$env:USERPROFILE\Documents"
        "$env:USERPROFILE\Pictures"
        # "$env:USERPROFILE\Desktop"
        # "D:\Projects"
    )

    # Backup destination (local, network share, or cloud-synced folder)
    DestinationRoot = "E:\Backups"

    # Backup retention (days) - older backups will be deleted
    RetentionDays = 30

    # Log file location
    LogPath = "$env:USERPROFILE\Logs\backup-log.txt"

    # Email notification settings (optional)
    EnableEmail = $false
    SmtpServer = "smtp.gmail.com"
    SmtpPort = 587
    EmailFrom = "your-email@gmail.com"
    EmailTo = "your-email@gmail.com"
    # Store password securely: Read-Host -AsSecureString | ConvertFrom-SecureString
    EmailPasswordFile = "$env:USERPROFILE\.email-cred"
}

# ============================================
# FUNCTIONS
# ============================================

function Write-Log {
    param([string]$Message, [string]$Level = "INFO")

    $Timestamp = Get-Date -Format "yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss"
    $LogMessage = "[$Timestamp] [$Level] $Message"

    # Ensure log directory exists
    $LogDir = Split-Path $Config.LogPath -Parent
    if (!(Test-Path $LogDir)) {
        New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path $LogDir -Force | Out-Null
    }

    # Write to log file and console
    Add-Content -Path $Config.LogPath -Value $LogMessage

    switch ($Level) {
        "ERROR"   { Write-Host $LogMessage -ForegroundColor Red }
        "WARNING" { Write-Host $LogMessage -ForegroundColor Yellow }
        "SUCCESS" { Write-Host $LogMessage -ForegroundColor Green }
        default   { Write-Host $LogMessage }
    }
}

function Start-Backup {
    param([string]$SourcePath, [string]$DestPath)

    try {
        # Use Robocopy for reliable copying
        $RobocopyArgs = @(
            $SourcePath
            $DestPath
            "/MIR"          # Mirror directory tree
            "/R:3"          # Retry 3 times
            "/W:5"          # Wait 5 seconds between retries
            "/MT:8"         # 8 threads for faster copying
            "/NP"           # No progress (cleaner logs)
            "/NDL"          # No directory list
            "/NFL"          # No file list (comment out for verbose)
            "/XA:SH"        # Exclude system and hidden files
            "/XD", "`$RECYCLE.BIN", "System Volume Information"
        )

        $Result = & robocopy @RobocopyArgs

        # Robocopy exit codes: 0-7 are success, 8+ are errors
        if ($LASTEXITCODE -lt 8) {
            return $true
        } else {
            Write-Log "Robocopy failed with exit code: $LASTEXITCODE" "ERROR"
            return $false
        }
    }
    catch {
        Write-Log "Backup error: $_" "ERROR"
        return $false
    }
}

function Remove-OldBackups {
    param([string]$BackupRoot, [int]$RetentionDays)

    $CutoffDate = (Get-Date).AddDays(-$RetentionDays)

    Get-ChildItem -Path $BackupRoot -Directory | 
        Where-Object { $_.CreationTime -lt $CutoffDate } |
        ForEach-Object {
            Write-Log "Removing old backup: $($_.Name)" "WARNING"
            Remove-Item $_.FullName -Recurse -Force
        }
}

function Send-NotificationEmail {
    param([string]$Subject, [string]$Body, [bool]$IsError = $false)

    if (!$Config.EnableEmail) { return }

    try {
        $Password = Get-Content $Config.EmailPasswordFile | ConvertTo-SecureString
        $Credential = New-Object PSCredential($Config.EmailFrom, $Password)

        $EmailParams = @{
            From = $Config.EmailFrom
            To = $Config.EmailTo
            Subject = $Subject
            Body = $Body
            SmtpServer = $Config.SmtpServer
            Port = $Config.SmtpPort
            UseSsl = $true
            Credential = $Credential
        }

        Send-MailMessage @EmailParams
        Write-Log "Email notification sent" "INFO"
    }
    catch {
        Write-Log "Failed to send email: $_" "ERROR"
    }
}

# ============================================
# MAIN EXECUTION
# ============================================

$StartTime = Get-Date
$BackupDate = Get-Date -Format "yyyy-MM-dd_HHmmss"
$TotalSize = 0
$SuccessCount = 0
$FailCount = 0

Write-Log "========================================" "INFO"
Write-Log "BACKUP STARTED" "INFO"
Write-Log "========================================" "INFO"

# Create timestamped backup folder
$BackupDestination = Join-Path $Config.DestinationRoot $BackupDate

# Verify destination is accessible
if (!(Test-Path $Config.DestinationRoot)) {
    try {
        New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path $Config.DestinationRoot -Force | Out-Null
        Write-Log "Created destination root: $($Config.DestinationRoot)" "INFO"
    }
    catch {
        Write-Log "Cannot access destination: $($Config.DestinationRoot)" "ERROR"
        Send-NotificationEmail "Backup FAILED" "Cannot access backup destination" $true
        exit 1
    }
}

# Backup each source folder
foreach ($Source in $Config.SourceFolders) {
    if (!(Test-Path $Source)) {
        Write-Log "Source not found, skipping: $Source" "WARNING"
        continue
    }

    $FolderName = Split-Path $Source -Leaf
    $Dest = Join-Path $BackupDestination $FolderName

    Write-Log "Backing up: $Source -> $Dest" "INFO"

    # Calculate source size
    $Size = (Get-ChildItem $Source -Recurse -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | 
             Measure-Object -Property Length -Sum).Sum / 1GB

    if (Start-Backup -SourcePath $Source -DestPath $Dest) {
        Write-Log "Completed: $FolderName ({0:N2} GB)" -f $Size "SUCCESS"
        $TotalSize += $Size
        $SuccessCount++
    } else {
        Write-Log "Failed: $FolderName" "ERROR"
        $FailCount++
    }
}

# Clean up old backups
Write-Log "Checking for old backups to remove..." "INFO"
Remove-OldBackups -BackupRoot $Config.DestinationRoot -RetentionDays $Config.RetentionDays

# Calculate duration
$Duration = (Get-Date) - $StartTime

# Summary
Write-Log "========================================" "INFO"
Write-Log "BACKUP COMPLETED" "INFO"
Write-Log "Duration: $($Duration.ToString('hh\:mm\:ss'))" "INFO"
Write-Log "Total Size: {0:N2} GB" -f $TotalSize "INFO"
Write-Log "Successful: $SuccessCount | Failed: $FailCount" "INFO"
Write-Log "========================================" "INFO"

# Send notification
$Status = if ($FailCount -eq 0) { "SUCCESS" } else { "COMPLETED WITH ERRORS" }
$EmailBody = @"
Backup $Status

Duration: $($Duration.ToString('hh\:mm\:ss'))
Total Size: $([math]::Round($TotalSize, 2)) GB
Successful: $SuccessCount
Failed: $FailCount
Destination: $BackupDestination
"@

Send-NotificationEmail "Backup $Status - $BackupDate" $EmailBody ($FailCount -gt 0)

docs.title: Step 2: Test the Script
docs.text:
PowerShell
# Run from PowerShell (as Administrator for best results)
Set-ExecutionPolicy -ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned -Scope CurrentUser
.\backup-script.ps1

docs.title: Step 3: Schedule with Task Scheduler
docs.text:
1. Open Task Scheduler (search in Start menu)
  2. Click Create Task (not Basic Task)
  3. General tab:
  4. Name: Automated Backup
  5. Check: "Run whether user is logged on or not"

  6. Check: "Run with highest privileges"

  7. Triggers tab:

  8. New → Daily → Set your preferred time

  9. Recommended: 2:00 AM or during low-usage hours

  10. Actions tab:

  11. New → Start a program
  12. Program: powershell.exe

  13. Arguments: -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File "C:\Scripts\backup-script.ps1"

  14. Conditions tab:

  15. Uncheck: "Start only if on AC power" (for laptops)

  16. Check: "Wake the computer to run this task" (optional)

  17. Click OK and enter your password

docs.title: Backup to Network Share
Backup to Cloud-Synced Folder
docs.text:
PowerShell
$Config = @{
    # OneDrive
    DestinationRoot = "$env:USERPROFILE\OneDrive\Backups"

    # Dropbox
    # DestinationRoot = "$env:USERPROFILE\Dropbox\Backups"

    # Google Drive
    # DestinationRoot = "G:\My Drive\Backups"
}

docs.title: Enable Email Notifications
docs.text:
PowerShell
# First, securely store your email password
Read-Host "Enter email password" -AsSecureString | 
    ConvertFrom-SecureString | 
    Out-File "$env:USERPROFILE\.email-cred"

# Then enable in config
$Config = @{
    EnableEmail = $true
    SmtpServer = "smtp.gmail.com"
    SmtpPort = 587
    EmailFrom = "your-email@gmail.com"
    EmailTo = "your-email@gmail.com"
    EmailPasswordFile = "$env:USERPROFILE\.email-cred"
}

Gmail Users

You'll need to use an App Password instead of your regular password.

docs.title: Script won't run
docs.text:
• Check execution policy: Get-ExecutionPolicy
  • Run: Set-ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned -Scope CurrentUser

docs.title: Access denied errors
docs.text:
• Run PowerShell as Administrator
  • Check folder permissions
  • Verify network share credentials are cached

docs.title: Task Scheduler not running
docs.text:
• Check "Run whether user is logged on or not"
  • Verify account has "Log on as batch job" rights
  • Check task history for errors

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Add compression (zip) for archival backups
  • Implement incremental backups with snapshots
  • Add Telegram/Discord notifications
  • Create restore script for easy recovery
  • Add pre/post backup hooks for databases

docs.title: Gardening & Growing
docs.text: Merge technology with horticulture. Discover automation techniques, monitoring systems, and data-driven approaches to optimize plant growth and cultivation.

docs.title: Topics Covered
docs.text:
• Smart irrigation systems
  • Environmental monitoring (temperature, humidity, soil moisture)
  • Garden automation
  • Plant growth optimization
  • Crop tracking and yield analysis
  • DIY gardening tools
  • Urban farming and hydroponics

Automate your garden and grow smarter with sensor integration and data analytics.

docs.title: Automated Garden Watering System
docs.text: Keep your plants happy with smart, automated watering based on soil moisture levels.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This project combines gardening with technology to create an automated watering system. Using a soil moisture sensor, your plants get water exactly when they need it—no more guessing or forgetting!

docs.title: Project Details
docs.text: Detail Information Difficulty Intermediate Time Required 2-3 hours Category Gardening + Automation Last Updated December 2025

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• Reading analog sensor data
  • Controlling a water pump or solenoid valve
  • Setting moisture thresholds
  • Basic data logging concepts

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Basic Arduino knowledge
  • Understanding of analog vs digital signals
  • Comfort with wiring components

docs.title: Hardware
docs.text: Component Quantity Notes Arduino Uno/Nano 1 Any compatible board Soil Moisture Sensor 1 Capacitive preferred (lasts longer) Relay Module 1 5V, single channel Water Pump 1 3-6V submersible pump Tubing 1m 6mm inner diameter Power Supply 1 For pump (separate from Arduino) Container 1 Water reservoir

docs.title: Software
docs.text: • Arduino IDE 2.0+

docs.title: Step 1: Wire the Soil Moisture Sensor
docs.text: Connect the capacitive soil moisture sensor: - VCC → Arduino 5V - GND → Arduino GND - AOUT → Arduino A0 (analog input)

docs.title: Step 2: Wire the Relay Module
docs.text:
Connect the relay for pump control: - VCC → Arduino 5V - GND → Arduino GND - IN → Arduino D7

Safety First

Always use a relay to control pumps. Never connect a pump directly to an Arduino pin—it can damage your board!

docs.title: Step 3: Upload the Code
Step 4: Calibrate Your Sensor
docs.text:
1. Open Serial Monitor (9600 baud)
  2. Note the reading in dry air (highest value)
  3. Place sensor in water (lowest value)
  4. Place in moist soil (target range)
  5. Adjust dryThreshold and wetThreshold accordingly

docs.title: Calibration Reference
docs.text:
Soil Condition Typical Reading Dry Air 800-1023 Very Dry Soil 600-800 Moist Soil 400-600 Wet Soil 200-400 Water 0-200

Sensor Variation

Every sensor is different! Always calibrate with YOUR sensor and soil type.

docs.title: Enhancements to Try
Sensor reads erratically
docs.text:
• Use a capacitive sensor (resistive ones corrode quickly)
  • Add a 100ms delay between readings
  • Average multiple readings

docs.title: Pump doesn't activate
docs.text:
• Check relay wiring (most are active LOW)
  • Verify pump power supply is adequate
  • Test relay with a simple blink sketch

docs.title: Over-watering
docs.text:
• Increase wetThreshold value
  • Add a minimum time between waterings
  • Reduce wateringTime

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Add LCD display for real-time readings
  • Implement time-based watering schedules
  • Create a web dashboard with ESP32
  • Add water level sensor for reservoir

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Capacitive Soil Moisture Sensors
  • Relay Module Guide

# Example usage
result = add_two_numbers(5, 3)
print('The sum is:', result)

Home Lab
docs.text: Build and maintain your own IT infrastructure. Set up servers, experiment with new technologies, and create a personal testing environment without limits.

docs.title: Topics Covered
docs.text:
• Server setup and management
  • Self-hosting applications
  • Network configuration
  • Virtualization and containerization
  • Backup and disaster recovery
  • Lab hardware recommendations
  • Security best practices

Transform your home lab into a learning playground for infrastructure and DevOps experimentation.

docs.title: Setting Up Proxmox VE
docs.text: Build the foundation of your home lab with this powerful, free virtualization platform.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Proxmox Virtual Environment (VE) is an open-source server virtualization platform. It combines KVM hypervisor and LXC containers on a single platform, making it perfect for home labs where you want to run multiple VMs and containers efficiently.

docs.title: Project Details
docs.text: Detail Information Difficulty Intermediate Time Required 1-2 hours Category Home Lab Last Updated December 2025

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• Installing Proxmox VE from scratch
  • Basic web UI navigation
  • Creating your first VM
  • Setting up containers (LXC)
  • Storage configuration basics

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Dedicated hardware (old PC, mini PC, or server)
  • USB drive (8GB+) for installation media
  • Basic networking knowledge
  • Understanding of virtualization concepts

docs.title: Hardware Requirements
docs.text: Component Minimum Recommended CPU 64-bit with VT-x/AMD-V Intel i5/Ryzen 5 or better RAM 4GB 16GB+ Storage 32GB SSD 256GB+ NVMe Network 1 Gigabit NIC 2+ NICs for VLANs

docs.title: Step 1: Download Proxmox VE ISO
docs.text:
1. Visit proxmox.com/downloads
  2. Download the latest Proxmox VE ISO Installer
  3. Verify the checksum (optional but recommended)

docs.title: Step 2: Create Bootable USB
docs.text:
Windows (Rufus)Linux (dd)macOS (balenaEtcher)
  1. Download Rufus
  2. Select your USB drive
  3. Select the Proxmox ISO
  4. Click Start
Bash
# Find your USB device
lsblk

# Write ISO to USB (replace sdX with your device)
sudo dd if=proxmox-ve_*.iso of=/dev/sdX bs=4M status=progress
  1. Download balenaEtcher
  2. Select ISO → Select USB → Flash

docs.title: Step 3: Install Proxmox VE
docs.text:
1. Boot from USB drive
  2. Select Install Proxmox VE
  3. Accept the EULA
  4. Select target disk (⚠️ This will erase all data!)
  5. Configure location and timezone
  6. Set root password and email
  7. Configure network:
  8. Hostname: proxmox.local (or your preference)
  9. IP Address: Static IP (e.g., 192.168.1.100)
  10. Gateway: Your router IP
  11. DNS: 8.8.8.8 or your DNS server

docs.title: Step 4: Access the Web Interface
docs.text:
After installation and reboot:

  1. Open a browser on another computer
  2. Navigate to: https://YOUR_IP:8006
  3. Accept the self-signed certificate warning
  4. Login with:
  5. Username: root
  6. Password: Your chosen password
  7. Realm: Linux PAM

You're In!

You should now see the Proxmox VE dashboard.

docs.title: Remove Subscription Notice
docs.text:
SSH into Proxmox or use Shell in web UI
# Edit the JavaScript file
sed -i.bak "s/data.status !== 'Active'/false/g" \
  /usr/share/javascript/proxmox-widget-toolkit/proxmoxlib.js

# Clear browser cache and refresh
systemctl restart pveproxy

docs.title: Add Community Repository
docs.text:
Bash
# Disable enterprise repo (requires subscription)
mv /etc/apt/sources.list.d/pve-enterprise.list \
   /etc/apt/sources.list.d/pve-enterprise.list.disabled

# Add no-subscription repo
echo "deb http://download.proxmox.com/debian/pve bookworm pve-no-subscription" \
  > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/pve-no-subscription.list

# Update packages
apt update && apt full-upgrade -y

docs.title: Enable IOMMU (for GPU Passthrough)
docs.text:
Edit GRUB configuration:

Bash
# For Intel CPUs
nano /etc/default/grub
# Change: GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet intel_iommu=on"

# For AMD CPUs
# Change: GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet amd_iommu=on"

# Update GRUB
update-grub
reboot

docs.title: Step 1: Upload an ISO
docs.text:
1. Click local storage → ISO Images
  2. Click Upload or Download from URL
  3. Upload your desired OS ISO (Ubuntu, Windows, etc.)

docs.title: Step 2: Create VM
docs.text:
1. Click Create VM (top right)
  2. General: Name your VM, note the VM ID
  3. OS: Select your uploaded ISO
  4. System: Keep defaults (BIOS, OVMF for UEFI)
  5. Disks: Set size (32GB+ for Linux, 64GB+ for Windows)
  6. CPU: Allocate cores (2-4 for most uses)
  7. Memory: Allocate RAM (2048MB+ recommended)
  8. Network: Keep default bridge (vmbr0)
  9. Confirm and check "Start after created"

docs.title: Create Your First Container (LXC)
docs.text:
Containers are lighter than VMs—great for services like Pi-hole, Home Assistant, etc.

  1. Download a container template:
  2. local → CT Templates → Templates

  3. Download: ubuntu-22.04-standard

  4. Click Create CT

  5. Configure hostname, password, template
  6. Set resources (512MB RAM is often enough)
  7. Configure network (DHCP or static)

docs.title: Recommended First Projects
docs.text: Project Type Use Case Pi-hole LXC Network-wide ad blocking Home Assistant VM/LXC Home automation hub Plex/Jellyfin VM Media server Ubuntu Server VM General purpose server TrueNAS VM Network attached storage

docs.title: Can't access web UI
docs.text:
• Verify IP address: ip addr show
  • Check firewall: pvefw status
  • Ensure port 8006 isn't blocked

docs.title: VM won't start
docs.text:
• Check if virtualization is enabled in BIOS
  • Verify enough RAM is available
  • Check storage space

docs.title: Network issues in VMs
docs.text:
• Verify bridge configuration
  • Check VLAN settings if using VLANs
  • Ensure DHCP server is reachable

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Set up backups with Proxmox Backup Server
  • Configure clustering for high availability
  • Implement ZFS for data integrity
  • Set up VLANs for network segmentation
  • Enable GPU passthrough for Plex/gaming
